acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] Lint catches direct CLI -> daemon imports
- [ ] Diagnostics include actionable replacement guidance
- [ ] No false positives in allowed modules
description:
**Problem**
Boundary violations can regress without fast feedback.
design: Extend lint rule to flag forbidden imports in CLI paths, with clear diagnostics and minimal false positives.
title: Harden dylint rule for CLI/daemon boundary violations

description:
**Problem**
Phase 1 implementation must be verified before Phase 2 starts.

**Context**
- REALTIME_PLAN.md §18: store discovery order, identity persistence, store-global lock enforcement
- Depends on: Phase 1 implementation beads + Phase 1 test infrastructure

**Design**
Deterministic tests for:
- Store discovery order: multiple repo paths → same StoreId
- Identity persistence: create store, close, reopen → same StoreId/ReplicaId
- Store-global lock enforcement: two processes → second gets error
- Namespace validation: valid/invalid NamespaceId patterns
- StoreMeta round-trip: write → read → compare

**Acceptance**
- [ ] tests/phase1_identity.rs exists
- [ ] Test: store identity survives daemon restart
- [ ] Test: lock prevents concurrent access with clear error
- [ ] Test: namespace regex validation (a-z, 0-9, underscore, length)
- [ ] cargo test phase1_identity passes

**Files:** tests/phase1_identity.rs (new)
title: Phase 1 tests: identity persistence + lock enforcement

description:
**Problem**
REALTIME_PLAN §8.4 requires HLC state to be monotonic per ActorId and persisted/restored per actor. Current daemon uses a single global Clock and only restores HLC state for the daemon actor, so custom actor_id requests can regress after restart.

Evidence:
- src/daemon/clock.rs implements a single Clock without actor partitioning.
- src/daemon/core.rs:564–570 loads hlc_state only for self.actor.
- MutationEngine uses Clock::tick for all actors (src/daemon/mutation_engine.rs:200–240).

**Why this violates plan**
Per-actor monotonicity and persistence are required; using a single clock and only restoring the daemon actor breaks this for alternate actor_id inputs.

**Acceptance**
- [ ] Maintain per-actor HLC state (e.g., map ActorId -> Clock state) and load from wal_index.hlcs for any actor seen/used.
- [ ] When actor_id is specified in a request, advance the correct actor clock state.
- [ ] Tests cover monotonicity across restarts for non-daemon actor_id.
title: HLC state is global; per-actor persistence not honored

acceptance_criteria:
- [ ] Epic decomposed into per model beads with dependencies and priorities
- [ ] Each model in beads_stateright_models/todo.md is implemented or explicitly scoped to non Stateright tests with rationale
- [ ] Each model asserts the invariants listed in todo.md and references REALTIME_PLAN.md sections
- [ ] Every model counterexample that maps to a product bug is translated into a deterministic regression test in the main codebase
- [ ] Documentation exists in beads_stateright_models (README.md or similar) explaining how to run each model and interpret counterexamples
description:
**Problem**
We need a tracked epic for the Stateright state machine model suite described in REALTIME_PLAN.md and beads_stateright_models/todo.md. Without it, modeling work is ad hoc and the sequence of invariants and model boundaries is not managed.

**Design Notes**
Use multiple small models at the seams: replication core, durability, idempotency, checkpoint lane, GC floor, identity and epoch guards, resource bounds, crash recovery. Each model should map counterexamples to integration tests.

**Files:** REALTIME_PLAN.md, beads_stateright_models/todo.md, beads_stateright_models/src, beads_stateright_models/examples
design:
**Design**
Build a model portfolio, ordered for maximum early signal, matching the todo list:
1) Realtime replication core: EVENTS, WANT, ACK, gaps, equivocation, monotonic seen map.
2) Durability semantics: applied vs durable watermarks, ReplicatedFsync(k) coordinator.
3) Idempotency and receipts: client_request_id mapping, PENDING vs COMMITTED, retry behavior, crash cut points.
4) Checkpoint lane: snapshot inclusion, included_heads, multi writer retry loop, truthful included watermarks.
5) GC markers and floors: ignore old events but advance contiguity and ACK.
6) Store identity and epoch mismatch guards across replication and checkpoint import.
7) Resource bounds and fairness: bounded buffers, round robin per namespace origin.
8) WAL crash recovery cut points: no origin_seq reuse, durable means survives crash.
9) Deterministic encoding and hashing: canonical CBOR and checkpoint hashing via property tests or tiny models.

Each model should live in beads_stateright_models/src or examples, keep state minimal, and reference matching sections in REALTIME_PLAN.md so counterexamples map to implementation changes.
title: Stateright state machine modeling for realtime Beads

description:
**Problem**
beads-rs lacks the Message issue type - ephemeral inter-agent communication beads that enable agents to send messages, coordinate work, and thread conversations. Messages are NOT exported to JSONL (Ephemeral=true) and auto-cleanup.

**What Message Beads Do (from Go beads)**
Message beads enable asynchronous communication between agents:
- Send work requests, status updates, questions
- Thread conversations via replies_to (now DependencyType)
- Ephemeral by default (not persisted to JSONL)
- Auto-cleanup when no longer needed

Message structure:
- Sender: Who sent the message (agent bead ID or entity URI)
- Assignee: Who receives the message
- RepliesTo: Parent message ID (creates thread via dependency)
- Ephemeral: true (skip JSONL export)
- Status: open → closed when acknowledged

**Go Implementation**
Type definition (internal/types/types.go):
- IssueType = TypeMessage (line 486)
- Fields:
  - Sender string (line 75): Who sent this
  - Ephemeral bool (line 76): If true, not exported to JSONL
  - Assignee string: Recipient
  - Uses dependencies table for replies-to threading

Ephemeral flag (types.go and nodb.go):
- Ephemeral=true skips JSONL export in storage layer
- Messages live in main database but not in issues.jsonl
- Enables short-lived coordination without polluting history

Threading (Decision 004: Edge Schema Consolidation):
- Old: RepliesTo field on Issue
- New: DepRepliesTo dependency type
- ThreadID field on dependency for efficient queries
- Enables conversation tree traversal

Cleanup:
- bd cleanup --ephemeral deletes old ephemeral issues
- Configurable TTL for message retention

**Design for Rust**
Data model (src/core/):
```rust
pub struct MessageData {
    sender: String,           // Agent/entity who sent
    assignee: Option<String>, // Recipient (optional)
    ephemeral: bool,          // Skip JSONL export
}
```

Add to Bead or use optional fields.

Threading via dependencies:
- Use DepRepliesTo relation type
- thread_id for efficient conversation queries
- src/core/dependencies.rs handles replies-to edges

JSONL export filter:
- Check Ephemeral flag in export path
- Skip ephemeral beads in JSONL serialization
- Ephemeral beads live only in database

CLI (src/cli/):
- bd create --type message --sender <agent> --assignee <agent> "Message text"
- bd list --type message [--label ephemeral]
- bd reply <message-id> "Reply text" (creates replies-to dependency)
- bd cleanup --ephemeral (delete old messages)

**Design Notes**
- Messages blur the line between data and metadata
- Ephemeral flag is critical - don't pollute git history
- Threading via dependencies is more flexible than parent field
- Consider: Message priority/urgency
- Consider: Broadcast messages (no assignee, multiple recipients)
- Consider: Message expiry/TTL
- Consider: Rich message content (attachments, structured data)

**Acceptance**
- [ ] Message type constant defined
- [ ] MessageData struct with sender, assignee, ephemeral
- [ ] Ephemeral flag implemented on Bead
- [ ] JSONL export skips ephemeral beads
- [ ] bd create --type message works
- [ ] Sender and assignee fields populate
- [ ] bd reply creates replies-to dependency
- [ ] Conversation threading via DependencyType
- [ ] bd list --ephemeral filters ephemeral beads
- [ ] bd cleanup --ephemeral deletes old messages
- [ ] Tests for message creation and threading
- [ ] Tests for ephemeral JSONL exclusion
- [ ] Migration from Go beads preserves message fields

**Files to study in Go beads:**
- tmp/beads/internal/types/types.go (lines 74-77: message fields, line 486: TypeMessage)
- tmp/beads/cmd/bd/wisp.go (ephemeral bead handling)
- tmp/beads/cmd/bd/nodb.go (line 225: ephemeral JSONL filter)
- tmp/beads/internal/storage/sqlite/schema.go (lines 33-35: sender, ephemeral columns)
title: Add Message issue type support

description:
**Problem**
`crates/beads-rs/src/test_harness/mod.rs` and a large share of `tests/integration/fixtures/**` still expose daemon/repl/WAL internals through the assembly crate. That makes local reasoning about crate ownership much harder even after the boundary split.

**Where**
- `crates/beads-rs/src/test_harness/mod.rs`
- `crates/beads-rs/tests/integration/daemon/**`
- `crates/beads-rs/tests/integration/repl/**`
- `crates/beads-rs/tests/integration/wal/**`
- `crates/beads-rs/tests/integration/fixtures/{daemon_*,repl_*,wal*,load_gen,tailnet_proxy,mutation,receipt,ipc_stream}.rs`
- owner crates: `beads-daemon`, `beads-daemon-core`, and `beads-rs` e2e tests

**Acceptance**
- [ ] `beads-rs::test_harness` contains only assembly/e2e helpers that cannot live in lower crates
- [ ] Daemon/repl/WAL fixture code moves to owner crates or owner-crate tests
- [ ] `crates/beads-rs/tests/e2e.rs` still has the support it needs without reintroducing broad subsystem ownership
- [ ] `cargo test -p beads-rs --features e2e-tests --test e2e` and relevant owner-crate tests pass

**Files**
- `crates/beads-rs/src/test_harness/mod.rs`
- `crates/beads-rs/tests/integration/daemon/**`
- `crates/beads-rs/tests/integration/repl/**`
- `crates/beads-rs/tests/integration/wal/**`
- `crates/beads-rs/tests/integration/fixtures/**`

notes.author: darin@book
notes.content:
Audit split:
- The scope under beads-rs-gou3.7.3 is not one local move.
- Child beads created:
  - beads-rs-gou3.7.3.1: move e2e harness from beads-rs into beads-daemon testkit
  - beads-rs-gou3.7.3.2: move repl and WAL integration suites into daemon-owned tests
  - beads-rs-gou3.7.3.3: classify remaining daemon integration helpers as assembly or daemon owned
- Reason: the audit showed three independent ownership moves with different target crates and verification surfaces.

notes.author: darin@book
notes.content:
Audit:
- workspace: /Users/darin/src/personal/beads-rs
- child beads closed:
  - beads-rs-gou3.7.3.1 move e2e harness into beads-daemon testkit
  - beads-rs-gou3.7.3.4 move WAL suite into daemon-owned tests
  - beads-rs-gou3.7.3.5 move repl suite into daemon-owned tests
  - beads-rs-gou3.7.3.3 classify remaining daemon-facing helpers as assembly-owned product integration coverage
- current-stack verification:
  - cargo test -p beads-rs --features e2e-tests --test e2e
  - cargo test -p beads-daemon --features test-harness --test wal
  - cargo test -p beads-daemon --features test-harness --test repl
  - cargo test -p beads-rs --test integration
  - cargo test -p beads-rs --test public_boundary
  - cargo check -p beads-rs --all-features
  - cargo fmt --all --check

title: Reduce beads-rs test harness to assembly and e2e ownership only
